title: Malaysian Elections
text:
Malaysia has a multi-party, first-past-the-post parliamentary system. It is a constitutional monarchy with a “federal structure with power nominally divided between federal and state levels, though in practice, it is highly centralised”.
In 2025, according to Department of Statistics, Malaysia had a multi-ethnic population of 34.2 million people, with 52.5% of the population being Malay, 20% being Chinese, 5.9% being Indian, 11% being non-Malay indigenous bumiputera groups, 0.7% being other races and 9.9% being non-citizens.
Prior to 2018, Malaysia had been considered to have one of the least honest and most manipulated electoral systems in Asia, ranking 142nd out of 158 countries assessed by the Electoral Integrity Project and falling into the “Very Low/Failed” category alongside countries like Afghanistan, Zimbabwe and Vietnam. A more recent report in 2025 believes Malaysia to be one of the most-improved electoral systems, moving up into a more middling category, ranking between Myanmar and Iraq.
However, the major issues facing Malaysia’s electoral system remain shockingly numerous: malapportionment (gerrymandering and vote dilution of urban and minority voters), lack of judicial independence, partisanship of the Electoral Commission and other institutions, racialisation of politics and Malay supremacism, restrictions on political speech and civil society, discrepancies in vote tallying, vote buying and clientalisation of key voting blocs, post-election manipulations, capture of institutional media by political parties and mass corruption.
We will be looking through election ballot data from all Malaysian elections to explore some of these issues to quantify them.



Since 1955, There have been a cumulative 2,794 federal-level and 7,206 state-level elections involving 14,595 candidates.
Legislative candidates are overwhelmingly male. Analyst Yong Pey Jung noted that it took 27 years (7 elections) for women MPs to rise above 5% of the total, and 44 years (11 elections) to surpass 10%. After 63 years of democracy, women MPs still form less than 15% of parliament:











Liew Chin Tong, a DAP politician, posits that 1990 was a watershed year in Malaysian politics as it was the beginning of a nearly three-decade long period of Barisan Nasional (BN) and UMNO dominance, largely led by Mahathir Mohamad. Strong patriarchal and patronage systems culminated in Mahathir Mohamad’s re-election as the world’s oldest Prime Minister in 2018.
The post-1990 election period was one dominated by incumbents and long-tenured politicians, with lower participation from newer political candidates.
Since 1990, 10 politicians have won 8 federal or state elections, including including Ku Li, who has won 12 federal and 1 state election in his career).











The long period of stability and ossification has resulted in politicians being more aged, as their (over)stay in parliament lengthens. This trend towards gerontocracy can also be noted in the plot below, which shows the average age of MPs increasing significantly since 1990.











The participation of Malay candidates in federal elections was at its lowest in the 1970s, and has been increasing every decade since, and are now higher than any time except for before the formation of the federation (when elections were limited to just the peninsula). Malay politicians’ participation in state elections, conversely, now at the lowest it has ever been, largely due to the large increase in Sabah and Sarawak bumi candidates.
The participation of Sabah and Sarawak bumis in federal elections was its highest in the 1970s, right after the formation of the federation, and has been decreasing ever since.
The share of Chinese candidates has been decreasing every decade. With regards to the population plot below, this seems to be largely in line with their decreasing share of the total population.

title: Malaysian Elections
text:
Data
The data for this report comes primarily from Thevesh, who compiled the Malaysian Election Corpus, containing federal and state-level election results since 1955.
Additional data, including the digitised census datasets come from JJean95
